source.title: Oscillatory Population-Level Activity of Dorsal Raphe Serotonergic Neurons Is Inscribed in Sleep Structure

confidence_rationale: Directly demonstrates in vivo that DRN 5-HT population activity is not monotonically low during NREM but oscillates at 0.01-0.05 Hz, with inverse correlation to EEG power fluctuations. This shows that NREM sleep is not a single 5-HT-off state but contains dynamic sub-states with alternating serotonin activity. Relevant to MECH-203 because it suggests the benefit-salience tagging signal (tonic 5-HT) continues to modulate in real-time during NREM, potentially creating windows of differential replay eligibility. Source quality high (Journal of Neuroscience, in vivo fiber photometry). Mapping fidelity moderate -- the oscillatory activity is characterised but its functional role in replay prioritization is not directly tested.
failure_signatures: Optogenetic and SSRI silencing of the 5-HT oscillation did not abolish EEG fluctuations -- serotonin tracks NREM states but does not generate them, weakening a causal role for 5-HT in organizing replay windows
The oscillatory 5-HT pattern during NREM is not shown to correlate with SWR occurrence or replay content
Mouse fiber photometry -- population-level signal, not single-unit; cannot resolve individual neuron subtypes or projection targets

mapping.source_claim_statement: DRN 5-HT population activity oscillates at 0.01-0.05 Hz during NREM sleep, inversely correlated with wideband EEG power (r < -0.7). Activity is highest during waking, oscillatory/descending during NREM, and minimal during REM. Silencing the oscillation does not abolish EEG fluctuations. NREM contains binary sub-states structured by 5-HT dynamics.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-203 requires that tonic 5-HT modulates replay eligibility during sleep. This paper establishes that DRN 5-HT is not simply 'off' during NREM -- it oscillates, creating dynamic windows. This is consistent with the benefit-salience tagging operation: if replay prioritization is gated by falling 5-HT phases within NREM oscillations, then the 0.01-0.05 Hz DRN oscillation provides the temporal structure for selective replay windows. The deepening troughs progressing toward REM onset also match MECH-203's SWS-before-REM phase structure: progressive 5-HT withdrawal marks the consolidation-to-recalibration boundary.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The paper shows 5-HT oscillates during NREM and correlates with EEG substates, but does not demonstrate that these oscillations gate SWRs or that episodes with higher benefit exposure are replayed preferentially during falling-5-HT phases. The causal direction is unclear: serotonin may track EEG substates rather than organize them.
mapping.source_context: In vivo fiber photometry in Tph2-CreERT2;Rosa-CAG-LSL-CoChR/EYFP mice expressing GCaMP in DRN 5-HT neurons. Combined EEG/EMG sleep scoring. Optogenetic and pharmacological interventions to test causal role of oscillation.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality high: Journal of Neuroscience, in vivo, rigorous fiber photometry with genetic targeting and causal manipulation. Mapping fidelity moderate: oscillatory 5-HT during NREM is demonstrated, but functional link to replay prioritization is not established. Transfer risk moderate-low: mouse, but DRN physiology is conserved across mammals.
